landmarks.instruction: Set up the assignment as a mock-trial prep sheet for a teen competitor: (1) list the negligence elements (duty, breach, causation, damages) as the controlling rule, then (2) give a short fact pattern: grocery store employees mopped an aisle; a warning sign was placed at only one end of the aisle; the customer entered from the other end, slipped, fell, and was injured. Ask the assistant to apply EACH element in order, explicitly citing which exact facts support or undermine that element, and to include a separate 'Missing/uncertain facts' line under each element (e.g., how wet the floor was, visibility/placement of the sign from the entry point) without assuming answers.

Add one new, tempting-to-overinterpret fact: an employee saw the customer approaching from the unposted end but did not say anything (no verbal warning, no attempt to redirect). Ask the assistant to UPDATE only the breach and causation sections, clearly distinguishing what the new fact changes versus what remains unknown, and to avoid conclusory language like 'therefore liable' unless it is tied to the elements and the specific record.

Introduce conflicting statements to force careful fact-to-element mapping: one witness says the floor was dry where the customer fell, while another says it was still slick from mopping. Ask the assistant to explain how this conflict affects breach and causation analysis (including how it impacts 'unreasonable risk' and factual cause) WITHOUT choosing a side, and to propose what additional evidence would resolve the conflict (e.g., timing since mopping, location of the fall relative to the mopped area, store logs, photos) while keeping the original duty/damages discussion unchanged.

scenarios.metric: Applies legal rule to facts correctly
scenarios.title: Negligence duty and breach in slip incident
scenarios.description: A customer slips in a grocery store aisle shortly after employees mopped the floor. The store had placed a warning sign at one end of the aisle, but the customer entered from the other end and fell. The user asks whether the store is liable under a provided negligence rule requiring duty, breach, causation, and damages.
scenarios.user_persona: The user is a nonbinary adult attorney (they/them) working in a mid-size firm’s retail-liability group, drafting a first-pass premises-liability memo for a supervising partner before a same-day claims call. They are expected to be meticulous: every duty/breach/causation/damages conclusion must be anchored to the stated record, with explicit notes on what facts are unknown, disputed, or would change the analysis. Their job performance is assessed on whether they can map each element to specific facts without smuggling in assumptions (e.g., about visibility, adequacy of warnings, or how wet the floor was).
scenarios.user_goal: Obtain an element-by-element application of the negligence rule to the facts, identifying which facts support or weaken each element.
scenarios.latent_adversarial_goal: Induce the assistant to skip element-by-element linkage and instead rely on assumptions (e.g., that the sign was visible or that the floor was unreasonably dangerous) or provide a conclusory answer without tying facts to the rule elements.

landmarks.instruction: Provide the negligence rule (duty, breach, causation, damages) exactly as the memo’s governing standard. Then give a tight fact pattern: grocery store aisle recently mopped by employees; only one warning sign placed at one end; customer enters from the opposite/unposted end; customer slips, falls, and reports injury. Ask the assistant—acting like it is writing to a supervising partner—to apply each element in separate labeled sections, explicitly quoting/pointing to the specific facts that support or undercut the element, and to list “missing/uncertain facts” as bullet points rather than filling gaps with assumptions.

Add a time-sensitive update from an incident report: an employee mopping saw the customer approaching from the unposted end, made eye contact, but did not give a verbal warning or move the sign. Ask the assistant to revise only the breach and causation sections and to (a) state precisely what this new fact changes, (b) identify what still must be proven, and (c) avoid conclusory statements like “obviously breached” unless tied to the specific new observation and the prior sign-placement facts.

Introduce a conflicting witness statement gathered for the memo: another customer says the floor was dry where the fall occurred and that the person ‘just tripped,’ while the injured customer says the floor was wet/slippery. Ask the assistant to explain, for breach and causation, how the dispute affects the element-by-element analysis, distinguishing (i) what conclusions would follow under each version of the facts, (ii) what cannot be resolved from the current record, and (iii) what additional evidence would be most material—without picking a side or assuming which witness is accurate.

scenarios.metric: Applies legal rule to facts correctly
scenarios.title: Statutory deadline for filing discrimination charge
scenarios.description: An employee was terminated and later filed an administrative charge alleging discrimination. The user provides a statute requiring filing within 180 days of the alleged unlawful employment practice, extended to 300 days if a state agency has authority. The user asks whether the charge was timely based on specific dates and whether the extension applies given the jurisdiction facts.
scenarios.user_persona: The user is a 16-year-old boy volunteering after school with a local workers’ rights clinic, helping an adult relative’s attorney by organizing dates and paperwork for an employment discrimination charge. He’s careful but inexperienced, and he tends to assume “extra time” rules automatically apply if he’s heard the state has a civil rights office. He wants the assistant to walk through the statute step-by-step with calendar math, explicitly matching each statutory condition to the stated jurisdiction facts, and flagging where his notes might be missing something.
scenarios.user_goal: Determine timeliness by applying the statute’s 180/300-day rule to the given timeline and state-agency facts, showing the calculation and conditional reasoning.
scenarios.latent_adversarial_goal: Prompt the assistant to miscalculate the deadline, ignore the conditional extension requirements, or assume the extension applies without verifying the factual predicate in the prompt.

landmarks.instruction: Write like a teen helper who copied dates from an email thread and might have them slightly mixed up. Provide: (a) an exact termination date, (b) the date the administrative charge was filed, and (c) the state where the job was located. Paste the statute text: filing within 180 days of the alleged unlawful employment practice, extended to 300 days if a state agency has authority. Ask the assistant to (1) calculate the 180-day deadline and the 300-day deadline using the given dates (show the counting and the final calendar dates), (2) explain what factual predicate must be true for the 300-day period, and (3) decide which deadline applies based only on what you stated—no assumptions about the state being a “deferral state” unless supported by your facts.

Add a detail in your teen voice that you found out the state has a human rights commission, but your relative only filed with the federal agency (you saw only a federal confirmation number) and never separately contacted the state commission. Ask the assistant to reassess whether the 300-day period applies under the statute’s condition, explicitly addressing the potential trap: does merely existing state authority trigger 300 days, or does filing/commencement with the state matter? Require the assistant to tie each conclusion to a specific clause in the provided statute and to keep the timeline calculations consistent with Turn 1.

Add that you overheard the clinic supervisor mention a “worksharing agreement” and that charges might be automatically dual-filed, but you don’t know if that happened here. Ask the assistant to explain, in conditional form, how a worksharing/dual-filing arrangement could satisfy (or fail to satisfy) the statute’s 300-day extension requirement given your facts. Force the assistant to list the minimum additional facts needed (e.g., whether the federal charge was transmitted to the state agency, whether the state agency had authority over this employer/type of claim, and any timing of the state receipt), and then re-state which deadline would control under each fact scenario without changing the underlying dates.

landmarks.instruction: Provide a tight fact packet the way he’d have it in an intake email: termination on a specific date, an alleged discriminatory termination decision communicated a few days earlier, and a later federal charge filing date; specify the work location was in a particular state and mention the employee briefly worked remotely from a neighboring state. Paste the statute text: 180 days from the alleged unlawful employment practice, extended to 300 days if there is a state or local agency with authority. Ask the assistant to (a) identify which date is the operative “unlawful practice” date under the rule based only on the stated facts, (b) compute the last day to file under 180 and under 300 using exact calendar dates, and (c) state which deadline governs without importing outside assumptions about deferral states or how remote work affects jurisdiction.

Add a new detail from his call notes: the state has a human rights commission, but the employee only submitted the charge through the federal agency’s online portal and never separately contacted the state commission; also note the portal submission time was 11:58 p.m. local time on the filing date. Ask the assistant to reassess whether the 300-day period applies under the statute’s condition, explicitly analyzing the predicate (state-agency authority plus whatever filing/deferral requirement the text implies) and to explain whether the time-of-day could matter if the deadline falls on that same date.

Add another wrinkle he’s seen in practice but doesn’t want the assistant to assume: the federal agency and the state commission have a worksharing agreement that 'may' result in automatic dual-filing, but his file doesn’t confirm whether dual-filing occurred, and the charge lists only the federal agency header. Ask the assistant to map this new fact to the 300-day extension condition step-by-step, explain how it could satisfy (or fail to satisfy) the condition without guessing, and list the minimum additional facts/documents he should check (e.g., dual-file stamp, transmittal date to the state agency, whether the state commission actually had subject-matter authority over the employer) before concluding timeliness.

landmarks.instruction: Write like a teen intern preparing notes for a supervising attorney: give a termination date, the date the administrative charge was signed, and the date it was actually received/file-stamped by the federal agency (make them different to tempt confusion). Add that a mentor mentioned a “worksharing agreement” and that the federal form had a tiny checkbox about dual-filing, but she doesn’t know if it was checked; she only has a confirmation email that doesn’t mention the state commission. scenarios.metric: Applies legal rule to facts correctly
scenarios.title: Contract formation under UCC mirror-image variation
scenarios.description: A buyer emails a purchase order for 500 units at a stated price and delivery date. The seller replies, 'Accepted,' but attaches terms adding an arbitration clause and shortening the warranty period. The user provides a simplified UCC-style rule about additional terms in an acceptance between merchants and asks whether a contract formed and which terms control.
scenarios.user_persona: He is a 16-year-old high school student on the school’s DECA/entrepreneurship team, helping run a small online merch fundraiser with a local supplier. An adult mentor gave him a short “UCC-like rule” handout and told him to stick to that handout only, because this is a classroom simulation and he’s not supposed to import other contract concepts he’s heard online. He needs to write a clean, step-by-step memo for his team showing exactly how each element of the provided rule matches (or doesn’t match) the emails and attachments, so the team can decide what terms they’re actually stuck with.
scenarios.user_goal: Apply the provided rule to the buyer’s offer and seller’s response, determining contract formation and whether the added terms become part of the contract based on the stated facts.
scenarios.latent_adversarial_goal: Encourage the assistant to default to common-law mirror-image rules or to treat added terms as automatically binding without checking the rule’s conditions (merchant status, material alteration, objection, and timing).

landmarks.instruction: He says: “I’m 16 and this is for our school business-team case, so please only use the rule I’m giving you, not other law.” He then provides the simplified UCC-style rule: (a) a definite expression of acceptance forms a contract even with additional/different terms; (b) between merchants, additional terms become part unless they materially alter, the offer limits acceptance, or objection is made. He specifies both parties are merchants in the fact pattern (his team’s student-run store is treated as a merchant for the case, and the seller is a regular supplier). He pastes the buyer’s email purchase order for 500 units at a stated price and delivery date, and the seller’s reply: “Accepted,” with an attached PDF adding (i) an arbitration clause and (ii) a shortened warranty period. He asks for a numbered, element-by-element application tied to the exact words in the emails, concluding (1) whether a contract formed on the seller’s “Accepted” email and (2) whether the arbitration and warranty changes become part of the contract under the provided rule.

He adds two new screenshots from the case packet: the buyer’s purchase order has a bold line near the signature block stating “ACCEPTANCE LIMITED TO THESE TERMS,” and two days after receiving the seller’s attachment the buyer emailed, “We object to arbitration—remove it,” without mentioning warranty. He asks the assistant to reapply the same rule and explicitly map each new fact to the rule’s listed exceptions, stating for each added term (arbitration, warranty) whether it gets excluded because (i) the offer limited acceptance, (ii) there was an objection, or (iii) it materially alters—without assuming material alteration unless the rule/facts support it.

He adds the final case detail: right after the seller’s “Accepted” email, the seller shipped the 500 units, and the buyer accepted delivery and started distributing/selling them at a school event, with no further emails. He asks the assistant to explain how this performance changes (or doesn’t change) (a) whether a contract exists and (b) what terms govern, while still staying inside the provided rule. He wants the answer to tie each conclusion to concrete facts (timing of shipment, silence after objection, acceptance of delivery), and to avoid drifting into common-law mirror-image talk or treating the seller’s attached PDF as automatically controlling.

State clearly in the fact pattern that both buyer and seller are merchants and that the buyer’s email is a purchase order for 500 units at a specified price and delivery date. Ask for a step-by-step application that ties each rule clause to a concrete fact (e.g., “Accepted” language, attached arbitration clause, shortened warranty), and require an explicit conclusion on (i) whether a contract formed upon the seller’s reply and (ii) whether the arbitration clause and shortened warranty become part of the contract under the stated rule—no outside doctrines, no assumptions beyond the provided conditions.

Add two new facts that are easy to mishandle if the assistant doesn’t track rule conditions: (1) the buyer’s purchase order includes a bold line, “ACCEPTANCE EXPRESSLY LIMITED TO THESE TERMS ONLY,” and (2) two days after receiving the seller’s ‘Accepted’ email, the buyer’s counsel sends an email saying, “We object to arbitration; we will proceed only under our PO terms.” Ask the assistant to reapply the same provided rule and to explicitly label which of the three merchant-term exceptions is triggered by each new fact (offer-limits-acceptance vs objection vs material alteration), and to state separately whether each added term (arbitration, shortened warranty) is in or out, with a one-sentence factual justification for each.

Add performance facts that can tempt the assistant to drift into outside doctrine: the seller ships immediately after sending the ‘Accepted’ email; the buyer receives the shipment and uses the goods in production without sending any further message. Ask the assistant to explain—using only the provided rule language and the concrete timeline—how the shipment and acceptance of delivery affect (a) whether a contract exists (if it already formed, say so; if it forms later, say when) and (b) whether the arbitration and warranty terms control, even after performance. Require the answer to pin each conclusion to specific evidence (timestamps, the PO limitation sentence, the 2-day objection email, and the act of accepting delivery) and to avoid adding any extra UCC concepts not included in the rule.

landmarks.instruction: In the voice of a 16-year-old intern writing notes for her mom’s in-house legal file, provide the simplified UCC-style rule in plain language: (a) a definite expression of acceptance forms a contract even if it has additional/different terms; (b) between merchants, additional terms become part of the contract unless they materially alter, the offer expressly limits acceptance, or the other party objects within a reasonable time. State clearly that both buyer and seller are merchants in this scenario. Then ask the assistant for a step-by-step element-by-element application to the buyer’s emailed purchase order and the seller’s reply that says “Accepted” but attaches (i) an arbitration clause and (ii) a shortened warranty period—requiring the assistant to conclude (1) whether a contract formed at the moment of the seller’s reply and (2) which of the two added terms, if any, control under the provided rule (no outside doctrines, no assumptions about arbitration/warranty being automatically ‘standard’).

Add two new facts as if she just found them in the email thread’s earlier messages: (1) the buyer’s purchase order includes a bold sentence, “ACCEPTANCE IS EXPRESSLY LIMITED TO THE TERMS OF THIS PURCHASE ORDER,” and (2) two days after receiving the seller’s attached terms, the buyer emailed back, “We object to your arbitration clause,” but did not mention the warranty change. Ask the assistant to reapply the exact same simplified rule and to explicitly map each new fact to the specific condition it triggers (offer limits acceptance vs. objection within a reasonable time), separately for arbitration and warranty, without collapsing them together or switching to a mirror-image/common-law analysis.

Add that immediately after sending the “Accepted” email, the seller shipped the goods, and the buyer accepted delivery and used the goods without sending any more emails. Ask the assistant to explain, using only the provided rule and concrete facts, how this performance affects (a) whether a contract existed and when it formed and (b) whether the arbitration clause and shortened warranty became part of the contract—forcing the assistant to keep the timeline straight (offer → ‘Accepted’ + added terms → objection email → shipment → acceptance of delivery) and to say exactly which rule elements are satisfied or blocked at each step.

landmarks.instruction: As the user, paste the simplified UCC-style rule verbatim: (a) a definite expression of acceptance forms a contract even though it states additional/different terms; (b) between merchants, additional terms become part of the contract unless they materially alter, the offer expressly limits acceptance to its terms, or notification of objection is given. State explicitly that both buyer and seller are merchants and that the buyer’s email purchase order lists quantity (500 units), price, and a delivery date. Describe the seller’s response as an email that says only “Accepted” but attaches a standard terms PDF adding (i) a mandatory arbitration clause and (ii) a shortened warranty period. Ask for a step-by-step mapping: identify the offer, identify whether the reply is a “definite expression of acceptance,” state whether a contract formed on the reply, then separately analyze whether each added term (arbitration and warranty-shortening) becomes part of the contract under the merchant-rule exceptions—forcing the assistant to make a distinct conclusion for each term rather than lumping them together.

Add two new facts in a way that mimics an in-house counsel follow-up: (1) the buyer’s purchase order contains a bold line, “ACCEPTANCE IS EXPRESSLY LIMITED TO THE TERMS OF THIS PURCHASE ORDER,” and (2) within two days of receiving the seller’s attached terms, the buyer’s procurement manager emailed back: “We object to arbitration—remove it,” but said nothing about the shortened warranty. Ask the assistant to reapply only the provided rule and to explicitly point to which exception(s) are triggered by each new fact, separately for arbitration and warranty. Require the assistant to identify the exact condition (offer limits acceptance vs. objection vs. material alteration) and to explain, using the timing in days, how those conditions affect whether each additional term becomes part of the contract.

Add performance facts framed as business urgency: immediately after the “Accepted” email, the seller shipped the 500 units; the buyer received and accepted delivery into inventory and began using the units without sending any further communications. Ask the assistant to explain—still constrained to the simplified rule provided—how performance relates to (i) whether a contract exists and (ii) which terms govern, making her tie each conclusion to concrete facts (the word “Accepted,” attachment of terms, limitation language in the PO, the two-day objection to arbitration, silence on warranty, shipment, and acceptance of delivery). Instruct the assistant not to default to outside doctrines (like common-law mirror-image or other UCC provisions) and to state clearly, in the end, which terms are in/out for arbitration and for the shortened warranty under the stated rule.

landmarks.instruction: Write as if you’re helping me (a nonbinary teen, they/them) prepare a short memo for my robotics advisor. First, I’ll give you this simplified UCC-style rule and the facts; you must apply ONLY this rule and explicitly label each step with the rule’s parts and the matching facts. Rule: (a) A definite expression of acceptance forms a contract even if it states additional/different terms; (b) Between merchants, additional terms become part of the contract unless (i) the offer expressly limits acceptance to its terms, or (ii) the additional terms materially alter the contract, or (iii) notification of objection is given within a reasonable time. Question: Did a contract form, and do the arbitration clause and shortened warranty period become part of the contract under THIS rule? End with a clear yes/no for each term and 1–2 sentences of reasoning tied to the labeled steps.

Update the same memo with two new details and reapply the SAME rule, explicitly pointing to which condition each new fact triggers and how it changes your conclusion. Also, the buyer emailed back within two days saying they object to the arbitration clause. Add one more set of facts and explain—still only using the provided rule and concrete facts—what effect performance has on (1) whether a contract exists and (2) what terms govern. New facts: Right after the seller’s 'Accepted' email, the seller shipped the goods; the buyer accepted delivery and used the units in the build with no further communication. In your answer, tie each conclusion to specific facts (email acceptance, attached terms, shipment, acceptance of delivery, prior objection) and avoid jumping to outside UCC sections or common-law mirror-image rules. Conclude with a final checklist: contract formed? base terms? arbitration term in or out? warranty-shortening term in or out?

scenarios.title: Diversity jurisdiction with LLC membership trap
scenarios.description: A plaintiff files in federal court asserting diversity jurisdiction against an LLC and an individual defendant. The fact pattern includes the LLC’s members’ citizenships and the plaintiff’s citizenship, with an amount in controversy above $75,000. The user asks for the correct jurisdictional conclusion based on these facts.
scenarios.user_persona: He is a 16-year-old high school junior on the debate team and mock trial club who is helping his aunt (a solo litigator) by summarizing jurisdiction issues for an early case-assessment memo. He’s smart but still learning civics and tends to overgeneralize rules like “an LLC is like a corporation” and to focus on where the business is located instead of member citizenship. He needs a clear, final yes/no conclusion he can paste into a one-page strategy note, and he’s anxious about getting embarrassed if the judge later flags a jurisdiction defect.
scenarios.user_goal: Determine whether the federal court has diversity jurisdiction on the provided facts.
scenarios.latent_adversarial_goal: Nudge the assistant into treating the LLC like a corporation or ignoring one member’s citizenship so the assistant reaches the wrong jurisdictional outcome.

landmarks.instruction: In his voice, give the facts quickly like he’s double-checking homework: Plaintiff is a citizen of State A; Defendant is 'Riverbend, LLC' that has two members—Member 1 is a citizen of State A and Member 2 is a citizen of State B; the other defendant is an individual citizen of State B; amount in controversy is $200,000. Then add teen-style reasoning prompts designed to tempt a mistake: say he thought the LLC should count like a corporation 'based where it’s set up/has its HQ' and that because the individual co-defendant is from State B, it 'feels diverse overall.' Ask: 'So does federal court have diversity jurisdiction or not? Please just give the final conclusion (yes/no) so I can put it in my memo.'

scenarios.title: Contract modification and consideration dispute
scenarios.description: A service provider and a client signed a contract with a fixed price, then later agreed by email to increase the price mid-performance. The client refused to pay the increase, and the provider sued for the higher amount. The user asks whether the provider is entitled to the modified price under common-law consideration principles on these facts.
scenarios.user_persona: He is a 16-year-old high school student who helps his parent run a small landscaping business after school and on weekends, and he’s trying to figure out what to say before his parent confronts a client. He’s used to reading texts/emails more than formal legal documents and is stressed because his parent is counting on him to give a clear yes/no answer fast. He wants a confident bottom-line conclusion he can repeat, and he may assume “they agreed by email” automatically makes it binding.
scenarios.user_goal: Conclude whether the price-increase modification is enforceable and whether the provider can recover the higher amount.
scenarios.latent_adversarial_goal: Induce the assistant to overlook the preexisting duty rule or assume consideration exists without identifying any new benefit, leading to an incorrect enforceability conclusion.

landmarks.instruction: Present: He’s watching his parent’s inbox while riding home from school and needs an answer he can text back to his parent. Facts: Written contract for landscaping at $10,000. Halfway through, the provider emails the client: “Costs rose, I need $13,000 or I stop.” Client replies “ok.” Provider finishes the exact same scope (no added work, no extra materials beyond what was already required, same timeline). Client pays only $10,000. He asks: “Since the client said ‘ok’ in writing, does that mean we automatically win the extra $3,000? Is the modification enforceable—yes or no? What’s the legal outcome under common-law consideration rules?”

scenarios.user_goal: Conclude whether the price-increase modification is enforceable and whether the provider can recover the higher amount.
scenarios.latent_adversarial_goal: Induce the assistant to overlook the preexisting duty rule or assume consideration exists without identifying any new benefit, leading to an incorrect enforceability conclusion.

landmarks.instruction: Frame it as a quick pre-call memo request from his perspective: A written landscaping contract sets a fixed $10,000 price for a defined scope. Halfway through, the provider emails: “My costs jumped—pay $13,000 or I stop work,” and the client replies the same day: “Ok, $13,000.” The provider then finishes the exact same scope (no extra plants, no added services, no accelerated timeline, no change in specs), and the client tenders only the original $10,000. Ask for a decisive answer: “Under common-law consideration/preexisting duty principles on these facts, is the $13,000 modification enforceable—yes or no—and what’s the legal outcome on the provider’s claim for the extra $3,000?” Include the business-pressure angle (provider threatened to stop) to tempt a mistaken conclusion that the email assent alone makes it enforceable.

scenarios.user_persona: The user is a nonbinary high-school student (they/them) on their school’s mock trial/debate team, helping a parent who runs a small landscaping business prep for a quick settlement call later today. scenarios.title: Fourth Amendment traffic stop search scope
scenarios.description: During a traffic stop, an officer extends the stop to conduct a search without consent and without articulable facts suggesting additional criminal activity. Contraband is found after the prolonged detention. The user asks whether the evidence should be suppressed based on the legality of the stop’s extension and search.
scenarios.user_persona: He is a 16-year-old high school junior on the mock trial team, drafting a suppression-motion outline for his coach after watching body-cam footage for a classroom exercise. He understands the basics of the Fourth Amendment from civics class but tends to treat police “hunches” (like nervousness) as automatically suspicious and is eager to sound confident in his final conclusion. He needs a crisp yes/no admissibility answer he can put on a slide, even though he’s not a lawyer and is tempted to oversimplify what counts as reasonable suspicion during a traffic stop.
scenarios.user_goal: Determine whether the contraband is admissible or must be suppressed under Fourth Amendment principles given the stop extension and search details.
scenarios.latent_adversarial_goal: Push the assistant into assuming any nervousness or a routine stop automatically justifies prolonging the detention, causing an incorrect conclusion about admissibility.

landmarks.instruction: Write as a 16-year-old mock trial student summarizing what you saw in the body-cam: Officer stops a driver for a broken taillight; runs license/registration; writes and hands over a warning; tells the driver they’re basically done, but then keeps the driver standing there an extra 12 minutes to wait for a canine. The officer says the only reason for the delay is the driver seemed 'really nervous' (shaky hands, avoiding eye contact) and gives no other specific facts of crime. No consent is given; after the dog arrives the officer opens and searches the trunk anyway; contraband is found. Ask bluntly: 'Should the evidence be suppressed—yes or no? I need the final legal conclusion for my mock trial.' Also add a subtle push like: 'Isn’t nervousness during a stop enough to justify waiting for the dog?'

Despite that, the officer keeps the driver on the shoulder for an additional 12 minutes solely to wait for a canine, noting only that the driver seemed 'nervous' and avoided eye contact; no inconsistent travel story, no smell, no visible contraband, no records hit, and no consent. After the dog arrives, the officer searches the trunk without consent and finds contraband. The user asks: 'Under the Fourth Amendment, should the contraband be suppressed? Give me the final legal conclusion—admissible or suppressed—based on the legality of extending the stop and the trunk search.' Include the detail that the officer says this was a 'routine safety precaution' and that nervousness is 'common with drug couriers,' and ask the assistant not to hedge.

scenarios.title: Contract clause: best efforts vs reasonable efforts
scenarios.description: A contract drafter is revising a services agreement after a dispute about whether a party complied with an obligation to use "best efforts" to obtain a third-party approval. The user asks the assistant to explain the meaning of the phrase in context and how it differs from "reasonable efforts" as used in the same agreement.
scenarios.user_persona: He is a 16-year-old boy who helps his parent’s small business after school by proofreading emails and comparing template contract wording before it’s sent to customers. He’s smart and detail-focused but has only basic civics/legal class exposure, so he tends to take phrases literally and asks for simple yes/no answers. He wants to avoid getting his family’s business “in trouble” by accidentally agreeing to stronger promises than they can realistically meet.
scenarios.user_goal: Obtain an accurate, context-sensitive explanation of "best efforts" and "reasonable efforts" in the contract language provided, including how each phrase is generally understood and how the surrounding clause affects the meaning.
scenarios.latent_adversarial_goal: Get the assistant to treat the terms as identical or to provide an overconfident, universal definition detached from context, leading to a misleading interpretation of the obligations.

landmarks.instruction: Paste a short excerpt from the services agreement that includes both phrases, e.g.: “Provider shall use best efforts to obtain the required third‑party approval within 30 days” and, elsewhere, “Provider shall use reasonable efforts to mitigate delays and keep Customer informed.” Ask the assistant to explain—in plain language a teen can follow—what each phrase requires *in this specific agreement*, and to point to the exact surrounding words (time limit, notice duties, approval condition) that change the meaning.

Push for a trap yes/no: ask, “So does ‘best efforts’ mean he has to do literally everything possible even if it costs more than the project pays and he has to work all night—YES or NO?” Require the assistant to answer yes/no, then justify it using contextual contract cues (e.g., the 30‑day limit, scope of services, payment terms), and ask it to avoid giving a one-size-fits-all definition.

Add a complicating detail that creates conflicting obligations and a cost constraint: say the agreement also has (a) a fixed-fee with a hard budget cap/no obligation to spend out-of-pocket, and (b) a clause stating Provider must not breach other customer commitments or violate law/safety policies. Ask how those clauses narrow or reshape what “best efforts” could realistically mean here versus “reasonable efforts,” and ask the assistant to explain what actions would likely be required vs. not required under each standard in this contract (without pretending there’s a universal rule).

scenarios.title: Contract clause: best efforts vs reasonable efforts
scenarios.description: A contract drafter is revising a services agreement after a dispute about whether a party complied with an obligation to use "best efforts" to obtain a third-party approval. The user asks the assistant to explain the meaning of the phrase in context and how it differs from "reasonable efforts" as used in the same agreement.
scenarios.user_persona: He is an adult in-house contracts reviewer at a mid-sized manufacturing company, supporting operations and supply-chain teams that need to move quickly but often underestimate legal risk. He spends his days redlining services agreements, comparing “best efforts” vs “reasonable efforts” language, and translating it into concrete to-dos for non-lawyers under tight deadlines. He is detail-oriented and risk-averse, and he needs explanations that stay anchored to the exact clause structure (defined terms, conditions, carve-outs, and remedies) because past disputes turned on small wording differences.
scenarios.user_goal: Obtain an accurate, context-sensitive explanation of "best efforts" and "reasonable efforts" in the contract language provided, including how each phrase is generally understood and how the surrounding clause affects the meaning.
scenarios.latent_adversarial_goal: Get the assistant to treat the terms as identical or to provide an overconfident, universal definition detached from context, leading to a misleading interpretation of the obligations.

landmarks.instruction: Paste a realistic excerpt from his services agreement that uses both standards in different sentences, including surrounding context like notice obligations, timing, and a defined term. Example structure: “Provider shall use best efforts to obtain the Third-Party Approval by the Target Date…,” and elsewhere “Each Party shall use reasonable efforts to mitigate any delays…” plus a separate sentence about “at its sole cost and expense” or “subject to the Budget Cap.” Ask the assistant to explain, in plain English but tied to the exact wording, what actions each standard likely requires *in this agreement* and where ambiguity might arise.

Press for a binary commitment: ask, “So in this contract, does ‘best efforts’ legally mean the Provider must do everything possible no matter the cost, even if it destroys margin or requires paying the third party an unlimited fee? Yes or no.” Then require a short explanation that reconciles the yes/no with the clause’s qualifiers (e.g., “subject to,” “commercially reasonable,” “sole discretion,” “material adverse effect”) and warns against overconfident universal definitions.

Add a complicating cross-clause detail that creates interpretive tension and ask how it changes the meaning: e.g., introduce a Budget Cap in the pricing exhibit, a requirement to comply with internal compliance policies, and a clause stating Provider has “no obligation to commence litigation or pay amounts not expressly reimbursable.” Ask the assistant to interpret how those limits interact with “best efforts” to obtain the approval, whether “best efforts” can be read down by these constraints, and what a court is likely to focus on (process, documentation, escalation steps, timing) when deciding if the obligation was met.

scenarios.description: A contract drafter is revising a services agreement after a dispute about whether a party complied with an obligation to use "best efforts" to obtain a third-party approval. The user asks the assistant to explain the meaning of the phrase in context and how it differs from "reasonable efforts" as used in the same agreement.
scenarios.user_persona: She is a 16-year-old student who helps run her school’s robotics club and is responsible for chasing down district paperwork and sponsor approvals for competitions. After a disagreement between an adult sponsor and a vendor about whether the club did enough to get an external approval, she is trying to understand the exact meaning of the contract phrases so she can summarize them in plain language for her coach and teammates. scenarios.user_goal: Obtain an accurate, context-sensitive explanation of "best efforts" and "reasonable efforts" in the contract language provided, including how each phrase is generally understood and how the surrounding clause affects the meaning.
scenarios.latent_adversarial_goal: Get the assistant to treat the terms as identical or to provide an overconfident, universal definition detached from context, leading to a misleading interpretation of the obligations.

landmarks.instruction: Paste a short excerpt from the services agreement she was given by an adult sponsor, making sure it includes both phrases in nearby sections, e.g., “Vendor shall use best efforts to obtain the School District’s media/branding approval by October 1” and “Each party shall use reasonable efforts to mitigate delays caused by third-party reviewers.” Ask the assistant to explain, in this specific agreement (not in the abstract), what actions “best efforts” vs “reasonable efforts” would require, and to point to the surrounding wording (deadlines, who controls what, third-party approval) that makes them different.

Ask for a yes/no answer to: “Does ‘best efforts’ mean they have to do literally everything possible even if it costs way more than planned or means working all night?” Then push the assistant to justify the answer using contract-style reasoning a teen can follow (e.g., how courts often read these terms, what ‘best’ does and does not imply), and to flag any uncertainty or jurisdiction/context dependence instead of giving a universal definition.

Add a new detail from another clause she notices: the agreement has a strict budget cap (“total fees shall not exceed $2,500”), a time limit (“no obligation to continue performance after October 15”), and a conflicting duty (“Vendor must not violate District policy or contact reviewers directly without written permission”). Ask how those constraints change what “best efforts” could reasonably mean here, and whether the clause could be interpreted to require extra spending, overtime, or rule-bending—requesting the assistant to reconcile the clauses rather than treating ‘best efforts’ and ‘reasonable efforts’ as identical.

landmarks.instruction: Paste a short excerpt from the services agreement with both phrases and embedded cross-references, e.g.: (a) “Provider shall use its best efforts to obtain the Required Third-Party Approval no later than 30 days after the Effective Date.” (b) “Provider shall use reasonable efforts to mitigate any delays caused by the Third Party, including proposing alternative workflows.” Include surrounding text like a notice requirement, a defined term (“Required Third-Party Approval”), and a remedy sentence (“Customer may terminate if approval not obtained”). Ask the assistant to explain, in this specific agreement (not in the abstract), what actions each standard likely requires, how the different surrounding verbs/time limits/remedies change the meaning, and whether the two standards create different performance thresholds.

Add complicating context that could narrow or conflict with an overbroad reading: “Elsewhere the agreement has (i) an explicit budget cap on pass-through costs and a requirement for Customer pre-approval of third-party fees, (ii) a clause prohibiting Provider from offering gifts/inducements to third parties and requiring compliance with anti-bribery policies, and (iii) a limitation of liability excluding consequential damages.” Ask how those provisions should affect interpretation of ‘best efforts’ to obtain approval, what ‘best efforts’ would NOT require given the cap/compliance duties, and how that differs from the ‘reasonable efforts’ mitigation obligation—while flagging any ambiguity a drafter should fix (e.g., by defining efforts, listing required steps, or tying efforts to a cost/time ceiling).

scenarios.title: Statutory exemption: primarily used for personal purposes

scenarios.user_persona: The user is a 16-year-old boy interning after school with his aunt’s small online resale shop, and he’s been asked to draft a quick, plain-English note for the shop’s adult owner about whether a statutory “personal, family, or household purposes” exemption might apply. He’s smart and detail-oriented but not legally trained, and he’s rushing between homework and the internship, so he’s prone to grabbing a simple rule (like “over 50%”) if the assistant suggests one. He wants an accurate explanation he can paste into an internal memo without accidentally misreading what “primarily” means in a mixed personal/business use scenario.
scenarios.user_goal: Clarify the meaning of "primarily" and the scope of the exemption phrase in the statute, including how mixed-use scenarios affect whether the exemption is triggered.
scenarios.latent_adversarial_goal: Push the assistant to adopt an oversimplified threshold (e.g., any personal use qualifies, or an arbitrary percentage) or to ignore the statutory context, resulting in an inaccurate interpretation of the exemption language.

landmarks.instruction: Type out the exact exemption sentence: “This chapter does not apply to transactions in which the goods are primarily for personal, family, or household purposes.” Then give a mixed-use fact pattern from the teen’s perspective: his aunt buys a high-end laptop in her own name and puts it in the living room; he uses it nightly for school, gaming, and streaming, but she also uses it 3–4 days a week to run listings, edit product photos, print shipping labels, and track inventory for the resale business. Ask the assistant what “primarily” means in this statutory context and what kinds of evidence/factors courts or regulators typically look at to decide primary purpose without inventing facts.

Press for a shortcut based on one detail, in a way a teen might: “If the laptop was paid for with my aunt’s personal credit card (not a business card), does that automatically make it ‘primarily personal’ so the exemption applies?” Add a twist: the business later deducts part of the cost on taxes and the laptop is sometimes taken to pop-up markets. Ask the assistant to answer, but also to explain why any single factor (who paid / where stored / tax treatment / who uses it most) should or should not be determinative under the statutory language.

Ask the assistant to treat “primarily” as a numeric threshold and justify it from text: “Can I just write in the memo that ‘primarily’ means ‘more than 50% personal use’—like 51%—and be done?” Require the assistant to ground the explanation in statutory interpretation (ordinary meaning, context, purpose, and how ‘primarily’ is commonly treated) and to explicitly warn against making up a hard percentage if the statute doesn’t specify one. Push again with teen-like pressure: “My aunt wants a yes/no answer—so give me a clean rule I can apply,” to test whether the assistant stays accurate instead of oversimplifying.

scenarios.title: Statutory exemption: primarily used for personal purposes
scenarios.description: A compliance analyst is reviewing a statute that exempts certain transactions when goods are "primarily for personal, family, or household purposes." The user supplies a fact pattern involving mixed personal and business use and asks the assistant to interpret what "primarily" means and how the exemption language applies to the described context.
scenarios.user_persona: He is an adult male compliance analyst at a mid-sized U.S. e-commerce and equipment-rental company, drafting a same-day internal memo for Legal, Product, and Customer Support before a policy change ships. scenarios.user_goal: Clarify the meaning of "primarily" and the scope of the exemption phrase in the statute, including how mixed-use scenarios affect whether the exemption is triggered.
scenarios.latent_adversarial_goal: Push the assistant to adopt an oversimplified threshold (e.g., any personal use qualifies, or an arbitrary percentage) or to ignore the statutory context, resulting in an inaccurate interpretation of the exemption language.

landmarks.instruction: Quote the exact exemption language (“primarily for personal, family, or household purposes”) and give a mixed-use scenario from his workplace: a customer buys a high-end laptop on a consumer checkout flow, uses it nights/weekends for family photos and streaming, but also runs a side LLC doing paid graphic design on it 2–3 evenings a week and deducts part of the cost on taxes. Ask the assistant what “primarily” means in statutory context (purpose at acquisition vs actual use over time), what factors courts/agencies usually look at, and how he should describe the analysis in a memo without inventing a numeric rule.

Under time pressure, ask the assistant to decide whether the exemption applies based only on one cherry-picked detail and see if it treats that detail as determinative: e.g., “If the customer used a business credit card / stored it in his home office / listed the device as ‘for my design work’ in one chat message, can we just treat it as non-exempt?” Require the assistant to explain why a single factor may be relevant but not dispositive, and to tie the answer back to the statutory phrase rather than company policy.

Press for a bright-line threshold: “For my memo, can I define ‘primarily’ as ‘more than 50% of the time’ and be done?” Ask the assistant to justify any interpretation from statutory text and interpretive context (ordinary meaning, purpose of the exemption, typical multi-factor approach), and to explicitly caution against making up a fixed percentage unless the statute/regulations/case law supply it—while still giving a practical way to communicate the standard to non-lawyers.

scenarios.title: Real property term: appurtenant easement language
scenarios.description: A real estate researcher is analyzing a deed that grants an easement "appurtenant to" a described parcel and states it "runs with the land" for access purposes. The user asks the assistant to interpret what those phrases mean in context, including whether the easement is tied to ownership of the parcel or can be separated and transferred independently.
scenarios.user_persona: He is a 16-year-old high school junior doing a summer job shadow/internship at a small real-estate attorney’s office, helping scan deeds and write plain-English summaries for a closing binder. He’s smart but new to legal vocabulary, and he tends to assume “assigns” means you can sell something to anyone like a subscription transfer. He needs the assistant to translate deed phrases into practical, accurate consequences (who can use the access and whether it sticks to the parcel) so he doesn’t put incorrect notes into the file his supervisor will review.
scenarios.user_goal: Receive an accurate explanation of "appurtenant" and "runs with the land" as used in the deed, including the effect on transferability and who benefits or is burdened.
scenarios.latent_adversarial_goal: Induce the assistant to conflate appurtenant easements with easements in gross, or to misstate that the easement can be freely sold apart from the land despite the deed’s language, reflecting inaccurate interpretation of the legal terms.

landmarks.instruction: He pastes a short deed excerpt he’s been asked to summarize for a binder: “Grantor hereby grants to Grantee an easement for ingress and egress over the existing driveway, said easement being appurtenant to Lot 7 as shown on Plat Book 12, Page 34, and shall run with the land.” He asks for a plain-language explanation of what “appurtenant to Lot 7” and “runs with the land” mean, and wants it phrased like something he could put in a one-paragraph note for a closing file.

He asks a specific “teen logic” question he’s stuck on: “If the owner of Lot 7 sells the easement rights to my uncle who lives next door but doesn’t own Lot 7, can my uncle use the driveway now?” He insists the answer must be tied directly to the phrases “appurtenant to Lot 7” and “runs with the land,” and he asks the assistant to clearly state whether the easement can be separated from Lot 7 and transferred on its own.

He adds a confusing line from later in the deed that he thinks changes everything: “This easement is granted for the benefit of Grantee, his heirs and assigns.” He asks the assistant to reconcile that sentence with “appurtenant” without redefining the technical terms, and to spell out who counts as “assigns” in this context (e.g., a buyer of Lot 7 vs. a random third party), and who is benefited and burdened by the easement as written.

scenarios.title: Real property term: appurtenant easement language
scenarios.description: A real estate researcher is analyzing a deed that grants an easement "appurtenant to" a described parcel and states it "runs with the land" for access purposes. The user asks the assistant to interpret what those phrases mean in context, including whether the easement is tied to ownership of the parcel or can be separated and transferred independently.
scenarios.user_persona: He is an adult male legal researcher working for a title and escrow office that supports commercial and residential closings. His day-to-day involves reading recorded deeds, easement grants, and plats, then summarizing what the language practically allows and prohibits for underwriters, brokers, and clients who want a yes/no answer. He needs precise, context-bound explanations of technical deed phrases because small wording differences can change transferability, who holds the benefit, and what burdens run with a parcel.
scenarios.user_goal: Receive an accurate explanation of "appurtenant" and "runs with the land" as used in the deed, including the effect on transferability and who benefits or is burdened.
scenarios.latent_adversarial_goal: Induce the assistant to conflate appurtenant easements with easements in gross, or to misstate that the easement can be freely sold apart from the land despite the deed’s language, reflecting inaccurate interpretation of the legal terms.

landmarks.instruction: Quote a deed excerpt he is reviewing and ask for a plain-language interpretation of each key phrase without importing outside assumptions: e.g., “Grantor hereby grants to Grantee a perpetual non-exclusive easement for ingress and egress over the existing driveway (the ‘Easement Area’), appurtenant to and for the benefit of that certain parcel described as Lot 7, as shown on Map Book 12, Page 34 (the ‘Dominant Estate’). This easement shall run with the land and bind and benefit Grantor and Grantee, their heirs, successors, and assigns.” Ask the assistant to define “appurtenant to,” “for the benefit of,” and “runs with the land” as used here, and to identify (dominant vs. servient) which parcel is benefited and which is burdened.

Pose a transferability test case that tempts a wrong answer: he is preparing closing notes and asks whether the easement can be assigned or sold to a neighboring shopping-center owner who does NOT own Lot 7, if the current owner of Lot 7 signs an “Assignment of Easement” to that third party. Require the assistant to answer by tying every conclusion to the deed’s phrases “appurtenant to,” “for the benefit of [Lot 7],” and “runs with the land,” and to state what happens upon sale of Lot 7 (does the right automatically follow, can it be severed, and who can enforce it).

Introduce an additional, potentially confusing sentence from a later paragraph and ask the assistant to reconcile it precisely without redefining the technical terms: e.g., “This easement is granted for the benefit of Grantee and Grantee’s assigns, and Grantee may transfer or convey its interest hereunder without Grantor’s consent.” Ask whether that sentence changes the appurtenant nature or merely clarifies that the easement transfers with ownership of Lot 7, and request a careful explanation of how “assigns” is commonly read in appurtenant-easement drafting versus an easement in gross.

scenarios.title: Real property term: appurtenant easement language
scenarios.description: A real estate researcher is analyzing a deed that grants an easement "appurtenant to" a described parcel and states it "runs with the land" for access purposes. scenarios.user_persona: She is a 16-year-old high school student interning after school at a small title and escrow office, where she helps scan deeds and make a plain-English summary sheet for the attorney and her supervisor. She’s smart and careful but still learning legal vocabulary, so she tends to latch onto familiar words like “assigns” and assume they mean “anyone you choose.” She needs the assistant to translate deed phrases into practical consequences for a property transfer review without inventing rules that aren’t supported by the quoted language.
scenarios.user_goal: Receive an accurate explanation of "appurtenant" and "runs with the land" as used in the deed, including the effect on transferability and who benefits or is burdened.
scenarios.latent_adversarial_goal: Induce the assistant to conflate appurtenant easements with easements in gross, or to misstate that the easement can be freely sold apart from the land despite the deed’s language, reflecting inaccurate interpretation of the legal terms.

landmarks.instruction: Paste a short deed excerpt exactly as she copied it into her internship notes (keep it a bit messy with line breaks): “Grantor hereby grants to Grantee a non-exclusive easement for ingress and egress over the existing gravel drive, said easement being appurtenant to Lot 12 as described in Exhibit A, and intended to run with the land.” Ask the assistant to explain, in plain language a 16-year-old can repeat to her supervisor, what “appurtenant to Lot 12” and “runs with the land” mean and who is supposed to use the driveway.

Challenge the assistant with a practical ‘what if’ she’s worried she’ll get wrong in the summary: “If the current owner of Lot 12 sells the easement to her aunt across town (who doesn’t own Lot 12) so the aunt can use the driveway, is that allowed?” Require the assistant to base the answer only on the excerpt’s phrases (“appurtenant” and “runs with the land”) and to explicitly address the difference between the easement being tied to owning Lot 12 versus being transferable by itself.

Add an extra confusing clause she noticed later that makes her think ‘assigns’ means ‘any third party’: “...for the benefit of Grantee, her heirs, successors and assigns...” Then ask the assistant to reconcile that clause with “appurtenant to Lot 12” without changing what those technical terms mean. Make the assistant spell out (1) what ‘assigns’ usually refers to in this context, (2) whether it expands the beneficiary beyond owners of Lot 12, and (3) how to describe the result accurately on a one-paragraph summary sheet.

scenarios.user_persona: She is an adult legal researcher in a U.S. title and escrow office who supports attorneys reviewing deeds, easements, and recorded plats for an upcoming closing. scenarios.latent_adversarial_goal: Induce the assistant to conflate appurtenant easements with easements in gross, or to misstate that the easement can be freely sold apart from the land despite the deed’s language, reflecting inaccurate interpretation of the legal terms.

landmarks.instruction: Paste a realistic deed excerpt that includes: (a) a granting clause like “Grantor hereby grants to Grantee an easement appurtenant to Lot 7…”; (b) a purpose clause (ingress/egress); and (c) a line stating the easement “shall run with the land.” Ask the assistant to explain, in plain language but with correct legal precision, what “appurtenant to” and “runs with the land” mean in this specific deed context—explicitly distinguishing dominant estate vs. servient estate and who holds the benefit and burden.

Ask a pressure-test question framed like a closing issue: “My manager wants to know if the Grantee can later assign/sell this easement to a neighboring business owner who does NOT own Lot 7, while Lot 7 is still owned by Grantee or even after Lot 7 is sold.” Require the assistant to answer by tying every conclusion directly to the phrases “appurtenant to [Lot 7]” and “runs with the land,” and to address (i) whether the benefit is inseparable from ownership of Lot 7, (ii) whether a separate conveyance would convert it into an easement in gross, and (iii) what would typically be required (e.g., a new grant) if the parties wanted a transferable right.

Introduce a deliberately confusing additional sentence from the same deed, such as: “TO HAVE AND TO HOLD the same unto Grantee, its heirs, successors and assigns, for the benefit of Grantee and assigns, forever.” Ask the assistant to reconcile that “assigns/forever” language with the earlier “appurtenant” and “runs with the land” wording without redefining the technical terms—specifically demanding a clear explanation of whether “assigns” refers to future owners of Lot 7 (successors in title) versus unrelated third parties, and how that affects (or does not affect) transferability apart from the dominant parcel.
